All right, let's see everyone. I'm gonna drop the question in chat as best as I can and then I'll read it quickly. So this is from Karma about module eight. This may have been covered already, so please excuse it if it has. If you have people going through your value newslet or soap opera series, maybe even a story power promotion, but at the same time, you're also running a product launch series. Do you care that the person is getting the SPP and the PLS at the same time? Will this not cause some confusion? How can we do this efficiently? You don't wanna have people getting 12 different things at the same time. If you're doing a PLS, they should get the PLS. If they're in the story powered promotion, they should be able to get the PLS. So you just, I don't know, Andre, how do you handle this? Yeah, I mean, so the technical side of it is inside of module 11. It's all there. It'll show you how to do it. Depending on which way, depending on how you wanna do it. So for us, we don't do anything different. We don't prevent anybody from seeing one thing and not the other, but at the same time as whenever we're doing a PLS, we're not sending newsletters. Very rarely do those two things overlap, but we don't do anything to prevent somebody from receiving a newsletter. Should we be in the middle of a PLS at the same time? Although I can't remember offhand when that's happened. I think there may have been a time when a PLS has ended on a Friday, which is the same day that we send a newsletter. And so they have just received two emails on that day, which is fine because only a small segment of people are ever in a PLS. The rest of the world are not in it. So it's just those people that you're communicating with in the PLS and they're okay with it because you're communicating with them and they know when a newsletter goes out. So they receive the newsletter. Everybody else has only received the newsletter. But I think there's other nuances. So, I mean, there's a lot to unpack here. And if, the first rule of systems theory is that you have to define the boundaries of the system. And wherever you define the boundaries of the system is going to, like that has enormous consequences. So, for example, if we build a system where we have a Facebook ad to the landing page to an email series to an offer, and we put the boundaries of the system around the, and we want to evaluate the performance of the Facebook ad. And we just put the boundaries of the system for that analysis around the ad itself. Like how many people click on it? What's the interaction rate? What kind of social signals do we get through commenting, likes, et cetera? Like that changes everything. We can optimize the hell out of the ad and make no improvement to the system at all. We can actually make the system much worse. And this happens all the time, not to go off of attention about paid traffic. But when you focus myopically on an ad, for example, and you optimize the hell out of it, or maybe you're a little more broadly focused, and you focus on the ad and opt-in rate for lead gen, to the, and you can drive those numbers through the roof and actually make your overall conversion rates worse. So, the first thing we have to do with IEM is draw the boundaries of the system where they're most valuable. And that can't be at just the list size and conversion rate. Because if we don't know what went into getting that list, then we have no idea what could have affected that conversion rate. So let's say for the sake of argument, let's look at two different alternatives. And I don't know which of these are true. I assume neither of these is true to be clear. I don't know anything about how that list was generated. But let's assume that option one is we were to say, hey, there's this amazing thing happening, and we're going to give away all these great ideas. And by the end of the next week, you're going to have 10 new ideas about how to generate content. Click here to be on that list. They're not interested in a very expensive offer, which this offer that you're just talking about is. Compare that to you at the other end of the spectrum. If in generating that list of customers, you were to say, here's what we're doing. Here's what it's all about. It's the focuses on this product. And this product is not right. This course is not right for most people because A, it's a year-long program. B, it's very expensive. They can go through the whole list. So people who are now it's eyes wide open. So now that list knows what they're getting into before they add themselves to the list. So what I would often do in the past, this is not rocket science by the way, is if depending on what the front end was, the front end was video, the initial one, and people who stayed longer on that page and say 10 seconds, but didn't become leads or whatever it was, I might retarget them with a text version of the same content. Also conversely, do the same thing with video. If you retargeted people and there was text on the landing page and do the exact same retargeting with video, because just some percentage of people respond to one or the other better. It's a second bite at the apple. Does it make the difference between success and failure? No, but it does confirm this idea that some people respond more to video than they respond to text. Some people respond to text more to video, but I think again, you have to understand the context. Somebody signs up for a webinar and then they either attend or they don't, but what happens next is the same. All right, is there any would you recommend two? Yes, and maybe, well, again, it becomes the questions of just in time, like somebody can watch the webinar immediately or is it live once a week, twice a week? Because that changes the dynamics of things because in general, for a webinar, you want to have some kind of attendance intensification. So you want to do something and there's experts at this out there, Joel Irway is one of them, I'm trying to think of others, but you can find some great free resources on this too, but you want to have, if you're doing something by webinar, your very first priority is to get people on the webinar. And it's very easy to sign up for a webinar, much more difficult to actually put your ass in the chair and attend a webinar. So, and just in time is great for a variety of reasons or someone can just choose to watch it now. Scheduled is great because you can intensify by email beforehand and let them know what the value that they're going to get, one trick that I can't remember who did this, but I thought it was brilliant, which is you create a webinar study guide and it's only like two or three pages, but it's the whole thing, it's a PDF, but it's a marketing document. And it'll say things like, and it of course follows the webinar in sequence, but it'll say something like, the very most important thing about whatever your topic is, is dot, dot, dot, and then there are like three lines where you can write it in. So of course, when you look at it, it's a huge open loop. Like, well, wait a minute, I'm going to learn like the most important thing about XYZ, and then three ways to expand your, whatever the thing is about to maximize your profit. Then you've got one, two, three with lines, like they've got to write it in. So of course, when I'm looking at this document, I'm like, oh, wow, I'm going to learn all of these things because you're showing me the questions, but I have to go to the webinar to get the answers. That's a very, I don't want to say basic, it's a very time-honored, time-tested strategy to get people onto a webinar. So to get back to Denise's question, is it two different sequences? Yes, it has to be, but it's three sequences, unless it's just in time. And again, I get hung up on just in time because you have to get people on the webinar. And then after the webinar, that's the traffic cop, right? Someone who has attended the webinar has now has an entirely different set of ideas and information in their head that you can build on over the next X number of emails that lead to the next step. The person who has not attended the webinar doesn't have that and you can't take them to the next step. They're not a good prospect yet. So if someone signs up to attend the webinar and they don't attend, then pick a number, three, four, five. You can sort of look at this two ways. You could just build a relationship with them, like take a step back, like, well, they didn't attend the webinar. So I'm gonna stop talking about the webinar. I'm just gonna talk about, you're gonna create an RBS that establishes that empathy and authority. And then later, email four, five, six, whatever it is, mentions the webinar again. So you build a relationship and then you sort of return back to the webinar itself and say, because they've said no with their behavior, right? So you have to take that into consideration. People vote with their behavior. So when someone says, hey, I wanna do this webinar, but I'm not actually gonna do it. First of all, they've told you they're probably not a great prospect. But they may have just also told you, you know what, life got in the way. So take a step back and realize, someone raised their hand, they took a step towards you, but they didn't go the full way towards you. So how do you have a conversation with them? Right? And that conversation is different than someone who raises his or her hand. It says, yeah, I'm interested in this thing. And they actually put their ass in a chair and they go, watch the thing. Well, now you have a different conversation. You're much, if it's an hour long webinar, you're an hour ahead in your relationship with them. So how do you build on the relationship now? We can't assume, it's absolutely, there's no way in hell it's the same conversation with those two people, that they're not the same people at all. You know, I think in an ideal world, that this isn't true. Then a ton of stuff. I like webinars. I think webinars could be amazing. And whoever doesn't gets relationship building series to value newsletter with periodic references to the webinar. I think you can do that same thing and just remove the initial relationship building series, send them straight to a webinar. Because you're actually building a relationship in the webinar. But, and again, webinars, I'm not jumping onto any webinar live. That's just me. If I was a customer of RWE mail, I wouldn't be on this call now. I would be waiting for the replay. Because then I'd get to- You didn't tell anybody that. Come on. I'm down just, you know, this is, and I'm a good customer. I'll bust up all the time. So I'm just saying that there's different types of people that value their own time differently to other people. So for me, because I've got a lot of stuff going on and there's, you know, there's windows of opportunity for me to pay attention to something. And also I like to stack my attention around certain things. So, because I'm terrible at multitasking across different things about, you know, different ideas, different, whatever. I find that tremendously difficult. So I stack my things. So when I'm writing fiction, for example, I need to get into that mode and it takes a little while and then I'm in it and I've got to stay in it for as long as possible. And I can't do something else. So when it comes to webinars on any topic, it could be fiction or nonfiction or anything, but I'm joining them with the hope that I get a repay link. And if I don't get a repay link, it's done. I'm not gonna email people and I'll ask them for replay links, but, and I just won't, I'll just miss it. So. You'd like Denise's webinar though, because it's just in time every hour. So you can do whatever the hell you want. So you're perfect, whatever she's selling, you're perfect for it. He just said Denise, he buys stuff. So send Andre a link to your webinar. But I mean, I think it's an interesting, something just popped into my head here to think about what is a webinar? If we put it in art of email terminology, right? If we just try to figure out how it fits in using that terminology, it really is a relationship building series. I mean, that's, it's doing the same thing because you're taking somebody from who the hell is this person? And I don't know, like mildly to largely distrustful, right? And you're, you're making, you're establishing the foundations of a, of a relationship with that prospect. So in many ways, if we think about, like how does this fit into the art of email? It fits in the same idea of like somebody is signing up for an RBS, but specific to a course. So they've given implicit permission about whatever the topic of the webinar is, being of interest to them. So this kind of sets up this opportunity for webinar to relationship building emails to soft bridge to something to get them on the, on a consultative call. That's that, that lines up pretty well because the, a lot of the relationship building can happen in that webinar. So I think that model works. But again, it's, I still, I think there's a tremendous dividing line. Don't, you do not pass go. Just like, just, just like you don't pass go until you get a relationship building series. You don't pass go until you watch the webinar. That's, that's the deal. Someone can't, and think, I've seen this happen a million times. And I've had clients who was, you know, like, well, if they didn't attend the webinar, you know, what can we still, you know, how, how should we sell to them? And I'm like, you don't, and if people look at me like I've not looked them, like I had nine heads, like why wouldn't you do that? Because it's like, because why do you want a customer who has already said no to like the lowest barrier thing that you're going to have them do? Like what, what kind of customer is that going to be? Right? So, I mean, to get, if you can imagine someone's like, yeah, I can't be bothered to get on this webinar on a 45 minutes to hell with that. I'm not going to do this thing. It's going to be beneficial to me. Do you want that person on a consultative call? Hell no. I don't want to try to sell that person. So I think the webinar is the gate that all good prospects have to pass through. And if they don't do it immediately, then the relationship building series, it's a, whatever, seven emails gets that it really builds, you know, it's empathy, it's credibility. You mentioned the webinar again at the end. You know, I wouldn't mention it every single day, but I would, you know, first three emails, empathy, authority, credibility, maybe email for you, soft bridge mentioned again, the webinar value proposition of the webinar, the last three, you know, let's say it's seven parts, first three are, or six parts, first three are relationship building series, second three are continuing to build the relationship around what you're talking about in the webinar. And you send them back to, and if they go to the webinar, then they kick off the post webinar series. If they don't go to the webinar, they get your value newsletter over time, but they don't see it, they don't get to do anything until they go to the webinar. You just nurture that relationship over time. That's how I would do it. Yeah, let me unpack some of that, how I'm hearing that, because if a webinar is considered the same as a relationship building series or similar, we don't, you know, we, somebody Oksin, they receive an RBS and they get to the end and then something else happens. They'll need to get added to the newsletter loop or they'll get put in, you know, they can raise their hand to get put into something else or, you know, they go into a story of heart promotion or something like that. But we don't create, we don't create like a firewall at the end of an RBS and then delete all the unopens. So, I'm just thinking out loud, yeah. So why would we do that with a webinar? So maybe, maybe there would be these two RBSs and the one is, the one is, you know, go listen to the down webinar and whatever's a good open rate, so with emails, you know, anything, let's say 50, 60, 70% for somebody raising their hand for something is the good thing. I'm just trying to, I don't know what I said. You may have said something similar to that anyway. Yeah, I mean, you're jumping to the next part of the question, which is, you know, do you, what's the strategy to unsubscribe people? Strategy isn't really the first thing as it's unsubscribed people. Now, okay, all right. So, and again, iOS 15 just changes all of this, right? So, because the question that as it's asked is what's your strategy to unsubscribe people? I'm referring to people that don't open the emails. Well, we don't know accurately anymore who doesn't open emails. Thanks, Apple. So, and you know, this is the other thing too. This is something to be very careful with. And we all, you know, the Imperial we, we all fall victim to this, which is sort of two things. One, we tend to think everybody buys like we buy or interacts with information like we interact with information. And we tend to think that, and our opinions about what we're doing are clouded completely because we're inside the bottle trying to read the label. So, there are going to be people who for whatever reason, they see an ad, they see whatever they see your, whatever it is that sets up your masterclass webinar and they're like, hell yes, I need this. And they sign up and then something goes sideways for them, right? The car breaks down, the kid gets sick, whatever it is, but life gets in the way. Or they've got a million other things coming at them. They're really interested in this. It's on their radar. Some people, I may know somebody who does this, will do something, will sign up for something or will buy something. So, it becomes a placeholder because they know they need to do that thing later. I'm referring to myself here in case that's not clear. So, don't assume that the, because what we want to believe is that the person is staring intently at their tablet screen, their desktop screen, their mobile device. They've signed up, they're waiting with bated breath to hear the genius we're about to lay down on them in our masterclass. And they better damn well do it right now. And then as soon as they do that, they better do it. That's just not how life works. So, to assume, this is no different than assuming that your window to make a sale closes really fast. Data is very clear, that's not true. People buy when they're ready to buy, not when you're ready to sell. So, the same thing is true in this context with a webinar, that you're gonna have plenty of people who, for all the right reasons, sign up, they expect to watch it at the top of the hour, and then life gets in the way and they don't. And then they get the first email from you in a second. And maybe they read one, maybe they don't. Maybe they don't read any of them that week that they get. I would say they get seven over seven days and they don't read a damn thing. And you think to yourself, you know what, screw you, you're out of here, buddy, right? But that person, again, maybe next, and maybe they never read those emails. But then your first newsletter comes in two weeks later and they're like, oh, shit, right. I signed up for that thing before. I need to look at that, but they don't. Life gets in the way again, context, whatever it is. But then they get your next newsletter. And there's a tidbit in that one and you build a little goodwill with them. And then weeks go by, maybe they don't open another newsletter, but five months from now, that person finally says, oh my, I have to solve this problem that I originally identified five months ago when I signed up for that webinar. But you've had five months when you've just been sharing value, establishing yourself as a preeminent authority in that space. And now they're ready. And when they then think to themselves, oh, now I gotta deal with this now, right? Whatever it is. Now I gotta finally sit down and deal with this. Who are they going to go to? The brand new person who they find, they stumble on in Facebook, or are they going to go to the person who has been building a relationship with them, kindly, gently, tirelessly over five months? Of course they're gonna go back to you. So don't assume the data is overwhelming on this. A very small minority of people who are going to respond positively are going to do that in a short timeframe and just build the damn relationship and reap the benefits. You just don't know when you're going to get them, but you can trust that you're going to get them. So there, that's my thought. Okay. Well, let's move on, let me think. Now this is a fun question. All right, so these are, I guess we're in the other, yeah, Denise was in the other question. These are the remaining questions. There's a mediums question. My blood pressure is going up. It's after this question too. All right, deep breaths. I'm gonna have you answer this question. Next question first. Deep breaths, deep breaths. Do some yoga while you're doing this so I can calm myself. Okay, here's Kim's question. This isn't the question I'm gonna lose my mind about to be clear. This is Kim's question. I'd love to hear more on how you think about creating your manifestos and where they fit into the art of email. Thank you. All right, Andre, you're the manifesto guy. Yeah. Let me think about that. We don't stick to, I mean, you're not gonna go to school or university and learn how to do manifestos the way that we do it. What? No, no. No, no. We do them our way. We've got some of our frameworks on the site already. If you want a more formal way, I mean, and to be clear, your perfect one will probably be somewhere between our more organic version and a very structured version. Jordan Peterson has, he's unpacked his entire process for writing an essay. So an essay isn't necessarily, well, it's not a manifesto, but there are elements that, some structural elements that there may be similar. And he's got the whole thing laid out at essay.app. Might be worth looking at that. It's thorough. I mean, it is everything you need to write a compelling argument in the form of an essay. And so that's a good place to start. And then from that, you know, maybe read some of our frameworks on the site. I think other than that, I think our idea of sharing manifestos is, it's really partially us trying to externalize certain components of an argument that we can then just share with others. So it's not a sales page. It's just a sharing part of our philosophy that is connected very deeply with a certain product, for example. So with the sphere of influence, there's this idea of prospects are made and the quality of a prospect matters. So it's basically just our thinking in X many thousand words of why we believe that that is the way forward. So it's not a sales page. It's not doing the same lifting that a sales page does. It's really just a sharing something of value in a way that's kind of un-packed our arguments, but more thinking out loud, you know? So, and then we just put it into this thing that we call a manifesto. And that's kind of, you know, when we did the auto email one, we got to a point in the course where we couldn't go any further. And externalizing certain stuff in the manifesto was important because it allowed us to then continue with the course because we had said stuff already that we know people would have read, but it's stuff that we didn't want to put inside of the course. Its place wasn't to be inside of our course. It needed to be external to the course. And where could we put that? Well, that's the manifesto because every single person that ended up inside of our email really needed to have read the manifesto. And most, I think many people did. But, you know, Sean's the structure guy. So I'm sure he's got a very succinct answer for this. Yeah, thanks. No pressure there. Pretty much that. Yeah, so first book recommendation is, it's a book called Abraham Lincoln and the structure of Reason. Author's name is David Hirsch. So this was a weird experience for me. When I realized how Abraham Lincoln sort of taught himself to think, I realized that's how I think. Now to be clear, I'm not saying I'm, you know, I think like Abraham Lincoln, it's not one of my point is, but the key part of that title is the structure of Reason. So that's, I've said on every, I think every call, and I say this all the time, I'm a very structural thinker. Andre is Anne Lamott. I'm John McPhee when we talk about writing. From a perceptual standpoint. So when I think about a manifesto, the manifesto for me is very similar to the way that John McPhee talks about the lead. Like McPhee can't write, his words, he can't write anything until he has a solid lead, on the same way. And where's the lead? Well, if you're creating a course, or if you're selling, or the lead often is in the manifesto, right? Like that's where you're setting up, you're making the case. And I tend to think like, and I've been called hyper-rational, and not, which I thought was a compliment, the person who called me that, and I realized that it was not meant as a compliment. So keep that in mind. But I might, the way I think, I would say it's hyper-structural. So for me, a manifesto, the way, and I've written a pre-working with Andre, I've written a boatload of manifestos for clients, sent a lot of traffic to manifestos. And the way I've thought about it, this is not right or wrong, this is how I've thought about it, I'll sort of unpack, I hope I can unpack how I think about the manifesto, then why it fits into the larger thing. So the way I think about a manifesto, and the way I've written a bunch of them, is I start like sort of Stephen Covey's idea, I begin with the end in mind. So I imagine that there is this point at which, right before opt-in, the person who has read the manifesto needs to have a certain X number of beliefs that coalesce into a single belief. And once that, so from that single belief, then I have to essentially make my case like a lawyer. And I have to go back and say, okay, if this overall thing is true, what would have to be true for that to be true? Well, this would have to be true in this. And then I sequence them. And it's a very step by step by step, and that's really what the book is about, is a structural reason. If you get, if someone says, okay, I understand that, gotcha. Yep, I understand that, okay, great, I understand this, great. Okay, well, if you understand those three things, here's the logical conclusion of those three things. Like that's how I think about, that's the role of the manifesto. No, is that how it's written? Not necessarily, like there's, the art of email manifesto has, we have characters who are introduced to, we introduce twice, and we sort of use the characters as vehicles, and we use a, there's sort of a structure to the manifesto that we rely on, like all of that's true, but we're making a case. And by the time you get to the end of that manifesto, we want to bring you to a point where you're not, the right people are nodding their head up and down. Like, yep, this makes a lot of sense. That's why it wasn't working, or that's why I've had this experience now, I understand. Like that's the thing. And we've mentioned before, Amanda, who's on this call, has an absolutely extraordinary manifesto on the front end. So go read that too, you can sort of see it in action. But it's, and I don't know Amanda's process. So it may be similar, maybe wildly different. I think there are all, many words lead to role in this example. But for me, it's, I can't, again, this gets back to the whole, like what's the lead? I can't do this stuff after, if I don't know what sets it up. And this is maybe just a blind spot in my thinking and my writing. And it's a very big part of how I think and how I write. Like I can't, I can't just start somewhere. I mean, I can from a thinking perspective, I do this all the time. Like I'll start with something, but then I have to put that something into a context. Like is this something foundational? Right? Or is this something like derivative? And it's 12 steps removed because what I'm always trying to do, and this is just how I'm wired. It's not right or wrong, it's just how I'm wired. I'm always trying to find a foundation to stand on that feels irreducible to me. First principles, like what is the thing that we can stand on? And in the art of email, one of the primary thing that we stand on is this idea that if you optimize for the short term, there's a whole set of things that you can do that make perfect sense until you realize that most of the selling that you're going to do is going to happen over a longer time period. Like if you accept this idea that you have this one opportunity to make a sale or a short window of time to make a sale, all of the decisions that we see being made in marketing make perfect sense. But the moment you realize that's not true, then nothing that people do make sense. So for me, I'm always trying to like find out what is true here? And if that's true, how do I build out from that so that the actions that I'm taking align with truth? This is a sort of a very esoteric way to think about this, but it's really this, it's something that was expressed by Buckminster Fuller. And Fuller said, essentially, and I won't get the quote exactly right, don't fight forces, work with them. So in the context of the art of email, we could fight this idea that people won't buy in the short term. We could do everything. And believe me, I've worked with plenty of clients who do. But at the end of the day, you can't fight gravity, right? You can jump out of an airplane and say, you know what, today I'm not a big fan of gravity. And that's going to be the last thought that you have as you rock it to the ground. Gravity doesn't give a shit if you believe in it or not. It just is. The fact that most people don't buy fast doesn't give a shit if you believe in it or not. It just is. So for me, like I wanna know, like that's where I wanna get to. And the manifesto is how I get there. And maybe it's my own crazy that's showing up. But for me, and the way that I've done it in the past, I think it's my approach. But I find it very useful. I will just, I have a document that I just call compilation. Whatever the manifesto is, dash compilation. And I just put ideas in there, quotes, ideas, thoughts, whatever it is. I guess over time, I just load it up. And then at some point I look at that, I'm like, okay, there's like 50 things in this document, but those 50 things, like I start to order, I start to put them together. Like this quote and this thought are really the same things. This quote expresses this thought, okay. So the 50 things aren't 50 things, they're probably 12 things. So I get down to a list of 12, that was just for the sake of what I'm saying, 12 things, okay. Here are the 12 things. What, like what's the big thing? Because what I want to do is I want to sequence them. And it's like, okay, what's the main point here? Like if these 12 things are all points that I could share, first of all, are they reducible? Can I combine it? Is there a larger idea here that I could put three or four of them into? So it's simpler because it's hard to make 12 points to somebody to get them to remember it. We want to try to be in the neighborhood three or so, three to five in that neighborhood, maybe seven. But I just, I'm trying to get down to that level. So then I can sequence it into a case. And then I become an attorney. And maybe it's all of this is because I wanted to go to law school and then I took the LSATs and I was not a chance in hell I was getting into law school, but I just tend to think like a lawyer. And I'm prosecuting my case with a manifesto because if I can get you to believe, because I believe it to be clear, I've made the case for myself, right? So if I can convince myself with an iron clad argument, it would have been like well-structured proof to myself that this makes sense, then I damn well should be able to convince you too. And if I can't convince myself, then I damn well shouldn't try to convince you either. So if we couldn't write that, the art of email manifesto, we had no business selling the course, plain and simple. Like if we couldn't make that case to you and say, and not to go on a side rant here, but it's two things with a manifesto. And this may be only important to us or me. I don't know, I think I'm batshit crazy when I say this, but it is so important to me that what we say in the manifesto is never violated by what we teach in the art of email. There's no daylight between the two. The art of email is the logical conclusion and the logical expression of the argument we've made and the case we've made in the manifesto without exception. And if we said something in the art of email that I could go back and look at the manifesto, like ooh, this isn't true, I couldn't do it. Like my brain would just shut down because I have such a weird, and Andre knows this, when we get into our occasional disagreements about how we mutually view reality, a lot of it is because I can't unclench my brain from something that feels like it's not structured rationally. It's so weird. Now, of course, I'll end on this note, which is people don't buy for rational reasons, people buy for emotional reasons. So that is a huge weak spot in my, if I were doing all the writing and only me, then this would not be nearly as good as it is. Andre is much better at putting the nuance and the emotion and all of that that he does makes the parts that I do infinitely better. And people buy because of emotion. And that's just fact. It's just an old idea, it's just oversimplified, but generally directionally right, that we buy for emotion, we justify with logic. But I'm not trying to sell with logic. What I'm really trying to do is make a case so that I can feel really good about the selling. Like I can explain to you, this is the situation in which this is going to be valuable for you so you know how to make a good decision. And this is where this thing we're talking about, it's into the world. Like all of that to me is just a way, it's a way to make my brain unclench in a way to be of service to our audience. That's the best way I can think about it. So it's a very long answer to the question, where does the manifesto fit in? I could not create a course without having the manifesto first. I just couldn't do it because the manifesto starts the conversation. It sets everything up and without the lead, I'm floundering like a turtle on its back, done. Okay, it's also worth drawing attention to the fact that manifestos and multi-page pre-sell starts, it's just different expressions of a very similar thing. Is we only want any product, there's an ideal customer for it. And part of putting people through these, a multi-page or a manifesto, is so we can draw out the people that are the best fit for the course, for the product. It's not about sucking everybody in. What can I say that's gonna resonate with 80% of the entire population of a certain market? That's not the point of it, it's to weed people out. And we can do that through the lens of a manifesto or a multi-page pre-sell start, because we only want certain people that believe that a certain way forward is the way forward. So for Art of Email, it's this idea that relationships is everything starts and ends with that. And when you have a strong relationship, everything becomes easier. Every promotion becomes easier. Everything, every newsletter that you send out, more people are gonna read it and pay attention to it. And that's all because of this idea of a relationship. Getting people into this course that don't believe that, they just believe sell hard and fast. That's so the manifesto pushes those people away. So that's it now, done. That's fun, that was good. That was an interesting side of it. Okay, so we gotta move on to Ian's question. I'm going to read the question. I'm going to have you answer it while I work on a breathing exercise. And after you answer it, I'm going to try to answer it without absolutely losing my mind, which the likelihood of that happening is so unlikely. Okay, here's the question. This is gonna be, my answer to this is just gonna be talking to a friend. So it'll probably be a little more raw. And that's gonna be a, here's the question. This is more of a general question. What's your deliberate practice routine like? How do you become world-class at email copywriting? How many years would it take? And you reckon? Well, the first thing we do is we get as many copywriting books as we can and we read and study them. And we put everything into it. I just thought you were gonna say we threw them out. We bought a copy of all the copywriting books and then we put those in the trash. That's not nice. Why would you say something like that? That's terrible. Well, first of all, we are not email copywriters. So let's get that first thing out of the way. We are just writers and we start by understanding and empathizing with the market with the emphasis being on the relationship. At that point, writing is just a matter of reading a lot and writing a lot. And then just expressing that in the writing. There's things you can do. Sean, well, I mean, you can say what you do with morning pages, but we do morning pages and we do various practices like that. But that's more, that's email, writing emails, that's a downstream effect of first showing up and just becoming a better thinker. And you can't keep things in your head. So you've got to externalize them onto paper. And then you just interact with your thinking through a pen and pencil and just get that clarity of thought about any idea, any concept, anything and everything. And Sean does it every single morning. I do it a version of that most days. I try to do it every, I try to do it as much as possible. And then, yeah, just read a lot and write a lot that we certainly are not email copywriters and we don't study copywriting books for the reasons Sean expressed in the previous video. And I certainly don't have a checklist. I don't think Sean does either. Maybe he does. Do you have a checklist? I'm gonna laugh at the idea that I never know when I'm gonna write or say it until I see it. Although I think the checklist can be a great tool. The Talgoon News book, the checklist manifesto is awesome. So there's like, it has two different types of checklists too. So I think a two review, one of them is a two review, two dash review, meaning the checklist is about things to review, which is good for like, you're gonna fly an aircraft. And it's just, I think you could do actually, but that would be interesting. I know we don't do it, but again, everything, it's easier for us because everything's easy with 20 years of experience. But I think certainly could have like a checklist. I wouldn't write to a checklist, but I might use one to go back and just sort of ask myself like four or five questions. It wouldn't be things like, you know, tactical stuff. It would be things like, you know, have I written this from the perspective of value for the audience first? Do I know the value I'm intended to, maybe I would phrase it this way, do I know the value? I'm communicating to my audience. Can you say it in a sentence? Like if you, someone said to you, I don't have time to read this email, what's it all about? In one sentence, can you tell them? This email is about XYZ. So can't do that, you know, what email's about. And that's a great exercise to do. And you can do it, you know, if you're an emergent writer like Andre, you might find out what it's about after you write it. If you're a deliberate writer like I am, you can't write it so you know what it's about. It's just, but, you know, it's, that's how I would use it. A checklist. All right, there's one thought I want to mention before, well, two thoughts I want to mention before I actually dive into the question itself and hurt myself. So, the first one is tactical, right? Good writing is good thinking. So spend more of your time learning how to think well and your writing will get better as a result. Maybe you can go the other direction with us. I mean, maybe to look, to learn to write better, you become a better thinker. I mean, Sonka Aaron's book, Take Smarter Notes, that's a great place to go at it. From the writing direction to be a better thinker. And that's such an incredible book. Just stupid, stupid, stupid, incredible book. You know, lots of books on how to be a better thinker. I mean, you know, start with Plato, work your way up from there. You know, kind of Plato, Socrates, Aristotle, that'll cover the big bases. Let's just ask one book, Take Smarter Notes, I think, Sonka Aaron's, S-O-N-K-E-H-R-E-S. All right, so the other thought, which I think may be helpful for everyone is to understand, there's a great, you've seen the movie 300. It's about the King William Ida and the 300 Spartans who stopped the Persian advance, the gates of Thermopylae, right? There's a scene in there where the 300 Spartans meet some Athenian warriors, and the Athenians are mad because they feel like they've brought more soldiers. The leader of the Athenian group says, we expected to see a lot more soldiers. What led you to bring so few soldiers? So Leonidas asks one of the Athenian soldiers, what's your profession? He says, I'm a potter. And he says, to somebody else, what's your professor? I'm a blacksmith. And then he turns to Spartans and says, Spartans, what's your profession? Right, and they all, they're all lawyers. So he says to the other general, because it looks like I brought more soldiers than you did. So I love this idea of Spartans, what's your profession? For all of us to actually be able to answer that question in one sentence, because mostly, because what you're gonna get answers that are so far away from what the reality is, like, oh, I'm a copywriter, yeah, Leon. Oh, I'm a email copywriter, bullshit. Right, like you're gonna get whatever. So I'll tell you what the answer is from my perspective. And I think it's the answer for all of us, just to be clear with everybody. I think I'm in the enthusiasm business, right? And not fake enthusiasm. I don't have any interest in spending my life getting wound up and trying to pretend like I'm excited about something and then trying to transfer that enthusiasm to others. That's just too damn much work. So because I'm in the enthusiasm business, the very first thing I have to do is I have to be enthusiastic about something. And the beauty of that is I have full control over myself. I'm a practicing stoic. So I try not to try being the operative word and practicing being the other operative word. I try to focus only on those things over which I have control. And I can control what I'm into, the things that I pursue, the attention that I give those things, the depth that I'm willing to go to, to learn more about them, where, how, and why I follow my curiosity. So at the end of the day, writing, thinking and writing is an expression of enthusiasm. And sometimes it's hard to write because I'm so excited about something. And here's a perfect example. Tomorrow you're going to get a newsletter that's going to, it's a two-part newsletter that's about creativity. And I wrote the base email for tomorrow and I mentioned to Andre earlier, I mean, I'm saying I wrote it as kind of hysterical. It wrote itself while I was sitting there with my hands on the keyboard, right? Because this is just something that's so important to both of us. That draft just emerged because it's just there. And because it's something we're so excited about. And it's leading to, we're going, we have a two-part series, each one has a hard bridge. Then there's going to be the week of Thanksgiving in the United States. There's going to be a promotion for our ideas to ask the updated version of our ideas to ask that's Masterclass that we created two years ago. I am so excited about this and so enthusiastic about this topic that it's like when I sit down to think about writing my hands are shaking. Because there's so much we want to talk about and we're so excited about it. So if I can't convey that enthusiasm through the written word, there's no book in the world that's going to teach me how to do that, right? The writing is the, that's the mechanism, right? If I try, but if I try to do it like, how do I get everybody excited about this thing that doesn't excite me? What? Like when you are excited about it, like reading Julie's SOS yesterday, it was very clear. She's excited about this. It just comes through in the words, right? How does Julie need to learn to write better? No, she knows just needs to think how to convey her enthusiasm, what she was doing in 26 pages. Like that's it, that's writing. It's just a transfer of enthusiasm with a written word. And if you're not, it's so, how do you learn to be a better writer? Get really curious, taking a lot of different, of information, let it percolate. We're talking about this on the next two emails or the four part process to creativity. And then when you sit down to write, just watch your fingers move on the keyboards and shit happens, it's amazing. It's how, and I'm not belittling how hard writing can be. You know, it's a hemmy way. You said all, you know, writing's easy. And you just sit down in front of a typewriter and bleed. Yes, writing can be hard, but when you're really excited about something, it's not that hard. And you can, getting better at the craft can be hard. You might push yourselves, but it's enjoyable hard. It's a good kind of hard. Okay, now let me answer the question as it was written. So here's the question again. What's your deliberate practice routine like? Here's the phrase in there that makes me wanna scratch my eyes out, which is deliberate practice. This is why I'm gonna go on a rant for a moment. So if you're not familiar with the term deliberate practice, it's a very specific type of practice that was identified by someone named Kaye Anders-Eriksson of University of Florida, sadly passed away not long ago. Kaye Anders-Eriksson is the person who Malcolm Gladwell grabbed the idea of 10,000 hours of deliberate practice. I thought like if it was blank or whatever it was, popularized the idea of the 10,000 hour rule. So first of all, the 10,000 hour rule is bullshit. That's not at all what Kaye Anders-Eriksson said in his research, Malcolm Gladwell, who I used to love as a writer and want to love as a writer, never had a single conversation with Kaye Anders-Eriksson when he wrote the book. He never checked how he thought about it with a guy who did all the research on it, which to me is unforgivable. It means I can't trust anything that Malcolm Gladwell has written. Like, and he's such a wonderful writer, but I can't read any of his books now. I can't enjoy it. Like the ones I read that I love now, I feel bad about because I wonder did he just kind of skate through the book and just write what he felt like made sense? Like in my previous conversation about it, for me, like needing to know that something is sort of true. It's just such a betrayal. So, and the other thing about this question, that's my conceptual rant, but the other thing about deliberate practice, that's really, really, really important to understand. It is a very particular type of practice and nobody does it. If you're not an elite athlete, you are not doing deliberate practice. What when people are talking about deliberate practice, they don't mean what Kaye Anders-Eriksson was talking about. When you actually dig in and see what he was talking about, you're talking about the 0.001% of the world who are virtuosos at something, who are in a very particular type of discipline that can be practiced a certain way. So, and Erickson, a co-author, wrote a book later called Peak, and this is where he distinguished what he, I believe he called purposeful practice. And purposeful practice is available to the rest of us. That's when we take something, and I see this a lot in the martial arts, where instead of teaching somebody an entire movement, you might break that movement down into its component parts, and you teach the parts, and you get lots of reps on the parts, which is focused practice. So, for writing, and to my friend Ian, I will tell you first and foremost, stop reading the deliberate practice research. Stop reading all of that because it's not what this is about. Writing is not a discipline that benefits from the model of deliberate practice, period. It does not. It does not fit the research. It does not fit how that framework was designed, and therefore it's the fruit of the poison tree. Everything that follows doesn't line up. So, think about writing. Writing is a lot more like jazz and improv than it is like playing scales. You can probably, and I think music was one of those disciplines that would line up well for deliberate practice, but you can probably play your scales and learn a song, and I think those are some of the examples that Eric's used using deliberate practice. But writing is jazz. Writing is improv. Writing is not playing scales. So, it doesn't benefit from that framework to think about. So, of course, now that I've said everything, I have not to think about it. The question becomes, how do you think about it? In general, and I think Andre has written this in other places, people who write well say the key to writing well is to read a lot. I think that's step one. You wanna be a better writer, become a better reader. And better reader means lots of things. Go listen to Sophie Bacall's interview with Tim Ferriss when he talked to me about these different writing frameworks. And if you wanna talk about the ultimate open loop, which still just makes me cringe when I say this out loud, and I've actually emailed Sophie Bacall and asked him if he's ever closed this loop and he hasn't, and it's making me crazy. But in the interview with Tim Ferriss, Sophie Bacall, I believe he said he has seven frameworks that he created for his writing, but he only gave four. And it drives me still to this day. This is years ago, drives me back shit crazy. I don't know what the other three are. But one of them, and he uses acronyms, but one of them is listening, it's, I can't remember what he calls it, but it's listening for the sound. It's listening to the sound of the writing. And another is much more technical. That's where I would start. Listen to how Sophie Bacall is a great writer, has broken it down, and then read the ways describing in those frameworks. That's a good first step. And you have to read a lot. You just, there's no way around that. So if you want to be a better writer and you have say a hundred units of time per week to do that, probably 70 or 80 of those units per week need to be in reading, maybe more, especially Ian's Young, especially for the first five, 10, 12, 15 years. Andre and I both still read a tremendous amount. That's the first key. It's a more deliberate reading, I think. Like you might read something and then, I'm reading Jordan Peterson's 12 Rules for Life. He does, the structure of how he makes his arguments is really interesting to me, and I don't really understand it yet. So I'm reading the book, it's one of those books that it's so good you can't, you want to get through it as fast as you can get, you want to savor it at the same time. So it's a difficult read in that sense. But then you can also do the same thing with Michael Lewis's book, because I'm very interested in writing narrative nonfiction. So you can read Michael Lewis and then, and read it, experience it, but then go back and say, how the hell did he do this? Right, like how did he create this chapter? Like, oh, he introduced his character. How? How did he introduce the character? Why am I so connected to that character? Like dissect it. When you read something you like, dissect it, and figure it out and start to make, like that's the, that's really, that's the practice, but it's not, it's not a practice where you break it down to this like list of I'm going to do these three activities. Writing just doesn't work that way, for whatever reason. Writing is more emergent, I think. So that, I think that's the answer. I don't feel like it's a great answer, but it's, I mean, I guess a lot, we have mentioned the journey, that keeping, this is important for everybody. When you read what we wrote, keep in mind that Andre is widely recognized as the, if not the, I think most people would say the, if not the, certainly in the top three email, email writers in the world, right? That's in, I would certainly put him as number one. So when you read our stuff, that's the first lens to think about. Second lens to think about is that, I was a professional journalist in DC for years, before I opened an agency. I have probably written professionally for 27, 28 years after a graduate career of writing. We had to unlearn now to write as an academic, where I wrote hundreds and hundreds of published pages as a graduate student. So I have 25, 27, 28 years of writing. It's not 10,000 hours, it's like 50,000 hours. So there's that. So when you read our stuff and you're like, damn, how did they do that? Well, it's 45 years-ish of experience. So just recognize that. Don't try to do that. Try to do your thing that you did yesterday a little bit better today. I look back at something like, I didn't like how that felt. All right, well, do a little better today. Do a little better tomorrow and keep doing that. And then you're going to wake up in five years, because five years are going to pass anyway, but you're gonna wake up in five years and you're just going to be infinitely better. Like there are so many books about writing. Oh my, I mean, you can, if you really, here's it, like here, we'll use the thinking idea of inversion to answer Ian's question. So if you're not familiar with inversion, the version is this incredibly powerful thinking tool, which is instead of trying to answer how to do X, you answer how to do non-X, and then you just avoid it. Right, so you, it's like, so the question, you know, the way that we could use inversion for Ian's question is like, if you wanted to never get better at writing, what could you do? Like you just make a list of things that you could do. And then you just make sure you never go there. And that's Charlie Munger's quote. You know, I just, the only thing I wanna know is where I'm going to die, so I never go there. So inversion here is a really powerful tool because if you really want to make sure you don't improve your writing, try to find the best books on writing and buy them and then try to get through them. And like, because there are so many and you can spend so much time procrastinating. I mean, you could easily, easily, easily spend the next three to five years learning how to write and not writing. I mean, just by buying the books, maybe go to master class and watch there are a bunch of good writing. Like you could study all of that stuff for years and not get any better at writing. His writing is an ass and chair discipline. And you don't get better at it until your ass is in the chair and the pen's moving or the fingers are on the keyboard. So Kim asks, I'd love to hear how you would think about a customer onboarding series in PLS when the purchase is a digital product. So I wanna, you should answer this first so I can think about it. I'm trying to understand the question first. All right, so I'll answer it because I have a, I think that I can explain it as I answer it because I, I'm thinking about it. I'm thinking about it because of the client that I worked with. So how would you think about a customer onboarding series in PLS when you purchase as a digital product? So I've worked obviously lots of clients with digital delivery. So PDFs, MP3 files, like remembering particular clients to like MP3 files for meditation, audio, downloadable videos back when that was a thing. So, the first thing of course that you deal with is that you, is delivery, right? So somebody buys something and immediately after their purchase, the onboarding series is, that's the first thing you need to do is get the thing in their hand. And generally that's where everything stops, right? I mean, you get that there's no onboarding, my experience is very little onboarding series. So I guess the first question I would ask is this, is this purchased, is this the beginning and the end, right? The alpha and the omega, you've sold them the thing and that's it, there's no more thing. Or is this purchase something that leads to other things that they can purchase? The reason I'm curious about that is if somebody is buying something and it's just digital fulfillment, then the focus of the customer onboarding series is just how to get the most value from what they purchased. Right, so it's email one, here's how to download your stuff. Here's what to do if you have any issues downloading things, let's let us know and we'll take care of it, it's timeframe, whatever. Email two is whatever the first thing you want to draw attention to, they've purchased, so you're building a relationship with a paying customer. So you're coming from this perspective of being thankful, sort of making it clear, hey, we really appreciate you're purchasing XYZ, over the next X number of days, we're gonna draw attention to a few things to make sure that you get the most value from it. The most, the feedback that we hear consistently is that this is the most impactful part of whatever you just downloaded and you can find it on page 23. And here's what so and so, here are three or four things people have said about that and why it's so valuable. Like that's kind of how you think about the customer onboarding series. It's just how are you attracting, how are you drawing attention to it? Now, if this is a purchase that leads to the possibility of buying other stuff, so maybe this is, it's, I'm thinking about this particular client that I worked with where you could buy, if you were interested in stress relief, you could get a meditation audio on stress relief, but they also had a series that there was one on better sleep and they had four or five things. And generally what we found are people who were interested in the product, and I think that's a good point that we found are people who were interested in one, were interested in all of them. So if that's the case, then the customer onboarding series has a couple of things to do. One, it needs to do the same thing a traditional one does, which is draw attention to the value that they just got. So if it's a meditation audio, let people know, how often, what are the common questions people have? Like, show us to this once a day, once a week, 10 times a day, like answer those questions. Like, again, you're just trying to show people how to get the most value for them from what they just purchased. These are reassuring them that they just made a good decision because in general, after people buy something, there's a little bit of buyer's remorse. Like, how is that stupid? We all do that naturally, and we're just offsetting buyer's remorse. Pat in the back, don't worry, you made a great decision, we're here for you. This is how, this is why it was a great decision. Here's how you're gonna get value from it. Here's how to, like, because you know all this stuff and they don't, and you're a teacher in that sense. You're just teaching them how to extract the most value from what they've purchased from you. If there's more value in the future, like if there's something else, don't send them an email on day two, it says, hey, I noticed you bought the sleep meditation audio. Did you know we have a stress relief one too? Let me come on, right, too soon. Talk about two, three days, four days, they're like, whatever, I mean, if it's a $9 audio, you probably don't need to get three or four days of how to get the most out of it, but give them a few days. Don't buy into this idea that they're gonna buy in heat and they just bought and get them to buy more. Don't be that person. But, you know, tell them about why the thing they just purchased is a good idea, how they can get the most value from it. And once they've seen that it's useful to them, then mention that there are other ways that they can get additional value. And if they're interested in that, I would do a hard bridge at that point, I would do a soft bridge. If you'd like to know more about our entire catalog, you can click here to see our other audio, our other meditation audios, or, you know, we have a five part series where we explain how the different, like how to integrate the entire series into your life in a way that's, you know, better sleep, better, whatever. Click here and we'll send you that series. Like that's an easy, you know, that's sort of an easy template to follow. You know, so I'm gonna hand it back to you, Andre, because there's another part of the question, which is how do you do the PLS? And I don't have a great answer for that yet. So I'm gonna let you, I'm gonna spend some time percolating while you're spending some time talking. I think one of the best things with the customer onboarding series is that, you know, someone's just made a purchase and you have the attention. And it's an opportunity to deline them in ways that is more than what they've just received. So one way to think about this is, is the customer onboarding series could be missing chapters. So that, so it's more, not the same, but, so it's not, you know, in chapter three, we discussed blah, blah, blah, make sure that you go and read chapter three, not that. It's different ideas or angles or ways of looking at whatever's in there, but through this lens of a missing chapter. So it becomes valuable all by itself, almost to the point that if they receive that onboarding series all by itself, without the actual PDF download, it would be as valuable, if not more valuable than what they've just purchased. So when you join those two things together, the two plus two, the experience that they're feeling is gonna be more than four. It's gonna be compounded. So I think that's a nice frame to use with a customer onboarding series. It's just, it's an opportunity to really delight somebody in a way that they won't expect or they weren't expecting. And then you can hand off to, as Sean mentioned, I think you could hand off to showcase series. So, we have a showcase series on insert topic. If you've invested in this, click here and we'll send it to you in a showcase series is really just showcasing something. It's unpacking it to the point where it's gonna be an absolute natural purchase for somebody without you trying to sell them on it. It's just you unpacking out of email, sphere of influence or something to a point that at the end of it, somebody knows precisely this is something for them right now or maybe later, but they've raised their hand to get that thing. So it's not a story apart promotion. I wouldn't necessarily attach story apart promotions to a customer onboarding series or that you can do anything you want. I would probably do showcase series and attach those, which is just an expression of a sub-mop series. All right, so the other part of this question is hard. It's hard, right? Jack would be a good person to answer this one, Jack born. So if I'm understanding the question well, which is how do you do a PLS when the purchase is a digital product, which I assume is not like, there's no open close, right? You're not saying we're taking this off the market forever, none of that bullshit. So I mean, there's an obvious answer and then a not so obvious answer. The obvious answer is if it's not time bound, then it's not a PLS, it's a story power promotion. So that may be the answer. If you want to do it as a PLS, which you could, right? You can do a PLS for anything if there's a reason to make it time bound, a legitimate reason for the record to make it time bound. And a legitimate reason might be something like, Black Friday is certainly a time when everybody discounts and there's some expectation. So if there's a, you know, if you're, I think I saw that you're serving the teacher market at some point. So if you're serving the teacher market, you could do a, I don't know when teachers buy their stuff. If it's at the end of summer, beginning of the, like at the budget, the other part of the year, I don't, it's just, I guess that part's irrelevant. But you can do time bound promotions with discounts if there's a reason to do them. And yes, you can make up reasons to do them. I'm on an email list with, I swear to God, every three days is the sum, insert, whatever thing sale. It's the most ridiculous and mind-numbingly annoying thing, but they, it's, you know, it's just giving people reasons to buy. I mean, I think in general, you have a business that benefits from a story power promotion, do that. And occasionally, if you want to, if there's a good reason to do something that is time bound, and it can be something, I mean, it doesn't have to be X% off. It can be something like, I mean, you may know for example, that, you know, an August sales swamp, right? That's kind of a truism. And you may do something where you say, you know, whatever for the, in the last week of August, you have this thing where you, you can, you know, normally all, you know, your courses or the thing, your digital products are $10 each individually, or you can buy all, you know, all 10 for $90, but because it's during the last week of August, once a year, you do the XYZ, Kim's board sale and you can buy all your stuff for $70. And it happens once a year, it's PLS. That's fine. Like there's nothing wrong with that. Just, I think when you make a habit of doing a discount PLS, then people just expect that you're going to discount. So you just can't use that all the time or you do have to use it all the time, but the other. That's how I've thought about it. Yeah. It's something Kim mentioned in the chat here. Done time bound with discounted launch of a new product. We do that, right? So it bolts the cannonballs idea when we're creating something new, we let people know this doesn't exist yet. And in exchange, you know, there's cohort one, we do this all the time. Cohort one gets a discount because this thing doesn't exist yet and we're kind of, you know changing the tires on the bus as we're driving it down the road. And because you're taking that risk with us, it's cost less. It's going to be more in the future. But we don't discount it in the future. We're true to our word. So yes, they're always, as long as the reason why feels right to you, then I think it's okay. Andre, your thoughts on this one. Yeah. I mean, I've done discounts a lot in the past and we've got to a point where I made a decision. This was before Sean joined that any released product is never discounted ever again. Cause I was having people that, and there's nothing wrong with it because I'm the one that actually did it, right? So on my birthday once a year, I would discount some products, like a certain product or all of them. And people could get at 50% off. Well, people that missed that or knew that I was going to do it, they would wait an entire year and not purchase and wait for the discount period and then bar one or multiple products on that discount period, which is fine because that's how I did it until I realized the stupidity, well, I mean, stupidity, it's just, that didn't, that seemed weird. I mean, the people were just doing what I'd, I was giving them an opportunity to do. So at that point is, okay, we're not doing discounts anymore ever for a release product. We do them when we, as Sean said, when we release, when we're creating something and we're interacting with somebody. So it's not a finished thing yet. That's different. And so it makes our Black Friday is far more challenging because it's not as easy as just saying, hey, he has 50% discount on insert product. We've, we create something from scratch or do something different. It just makes it harder, but it's more of a surprise we think. So I think you just need to make a decision that will be, you know, have your eyes wide open that if you do offer discounts, people will expect discounts and some will even wait for those discounts and will not purchase until there's a discount window and that will be their opportunity to make these purchases. But if there's no discounts, people are still gonna bar, they're just not gonna wait for the discount windows all the time. So just be mindful of that. All right, move on to the next question. Well, you know, multi-pages is just one expression of what's codified within Sphere of Influence, for example. You can have a single page that does the same thing, essentially, minus the click-through to the next pages. So we've got many multi-page things inside of the TLB world. If you click around, you can find them. Most of them are framed as manifestos. Some of them are three pages. And we're not presuming, I mean, in our expression of it, we're not presuming that we're gonna create a linear thing that is gonna fit with everybody. So if somebody goes to our homepage, which just, it's an expression of how we show up, you know, what matters for us. And then we leave it for them to do a choose your own adventure, which is, again, this is another expression of a multi-page. Some people might just click on the subscribe link or it's also linked to from the homepage contextually, and they'll end up over there, they'll read that, and that will become their two-step multi-page. Others will click through to our learn tab, which is our blog, and they'll go deeper. They might end up on a proper multi-page, which is one of our manifestos, we put many on there. And then they'll click through next, next, next, and that'll be this journey, this rabbit hole that they've just been swallowed up by. So there's no one way to do this. You can do it as a single pages, you can do it as a choose your own adventure style and allow people to find those pods themselves, which is super powerful. We get so many emails from people saying that they've read this page and that page and they've just got sucked in, and it's never the same. No one person has ever sent the same email where they've gone down the same pages, because it's a non-linear world we've created. And when you're starting out and you just wanna create a single path for somebody, sure, you can do a multi-page that then ends in an opportunity for somebody to get onto your email list. That's the easiest first step. It can be a single page like the Jonathan Boyd, they have to read 1600 words that's been structured in such a way that by the time somebody gets to the opt-in, they've had this insight and they want to play the guitar in this way that's been expressed by Jonathan. So they opt in and 70% do and then they'll bar and then they get introduced into Jonathan's world that way. There is no one way that one size fits all here. And I think over time, every business should turn into a world-building exercise, just like we mentioned about Brooklesteo, maybe in the last call or the call before that. But her world is non-linear, even though if you look at her homepage, it can feel linear. It's a sales page, there's a video sales letter, there's a button to bar, it seems very linear. But when you really look at the site, it is not linear. She doesn't hide all the buttons away. It's an expansive world that people can wander down in a way that matters to them. And there'll be insights along the way, people will become, there'll be a moment where someone says, I'm gonna become a customer before they click on a button and become a customer. And sometimes long before, it's hers is a $297 a month investment. So that moment that I'm gonna become a customer probably happens quite soon. Andra, you, show me some thoughts on this. Yeah, I mean, let me cover a few scenarios then. I think having a best hits. So between a relationship building series and adding somebody to your VNL, to your live VNL loop, you could have a best hits, which is, and the last thing with the best hits is you get to choose the newsletters that will take somebody down a path that exposes them to parts of your business in a way that's predictable, because you are in control of it. You know which emails they're gonna get. And let's say the best hits is one month long. It's a newsletter once a week. So four newsletters, maybe there's eight, if you wanna do twice a week or over two months. So then they get that. So now that person has a very deep relationship pretty quickly now because they've received these newsletters in an order that you want them to receive them in. At which point it can then move to your live loop. So when you're sending out the live ones, only people that have gone through the automated one then gets the live ones. That's one expression. I think that's a nice way of doing it. I probably wouldn't want people to be in an automated one for too long because then they're missing all the live ones where there's, which is mechanisms to introduce people to PLS's and stuff like that. You can schedule broadcasts, which is the scenario that Sean first mentioned, but then you can also create a sequence. So if you wanted to do this for an entire year, you could create a evergreen newsletter sequence and stack your one year's worth of newsletters inside of this. So you know people are gonna get it sequentially, but then you can use the automation that we share in module 11 that within the context of those newsletters that are asking people to raise hands. So it's got the hard bridges built into those newsletters that you know are going at a predictable frequency and it's exposing people to story-powered promotions. If somebody clicks to raise their hand for a story-powered promotion, for example, because you obviously in that scenario, you're not gonna be able to do PLS's. So it'll be story-powered promotion. Somebody raises their hand. The automation would unsubscribe them from the newsletter sequence. So it'll unsubscribe them from the evergreen newsletter sequence that would go through the automated promotion, they'll either bar or they won't bar. And then it'll subscribe them back to the same sequence. And the nice thing with the convert kit, I'm not sure about other ESPs, is it will insert them where they left off. So you can tell the convert kit to, that a certain sequence, people can only subscribe to it once. So once they get to the end of it, it's done. They can never resubscribe to it again. And that's a setting you can choose when you're creating your sequence. So if you said that people can only subscribe to it once, then that's perfect because when you resubscribe them to it, using the automation, once they've finished a story-powered promotion, it'll just put them back where they left off. So they'll just start to get the next newsletter, as if nothing had ever happened, which is perfect. So you could have an evergreen business that runs for a year plus, because whenever they go into these promotional sequences, it kind of pauses them in the newsletter side. Yeah, and you can run the business and make money while you're sleeping or doing whatever you're doing for a year. That's another way to do it. And I'll kind of break the open loop, but the first part is struggle, right? That's the work. It's taking all the stuff in. And that's a lot of, that's the first part of personal knowledge management. And interestingly, that's where most people stop. They just collect, right? It's just, oh, I read this book and there's this quote and there's this thing and they collect, collect, collect, collect, collect. Well, that's part of it. But that's a small part of the system. And what we really want to draw attention to is, yes, there's collection, but that collection, those inputs, the stuff we read, the stuff we watch, the stuff we listen to, percolates into ideas that then emerge. And then those ideas that are, emergent properties of the inputs also get jammed together and smashed into other things and bounced around with each other and with colleagues. And then something even more emerges from those. So it's, we want to draw some attention to that. So the first, my first thing I've already said, which I don't know why you would do that, so it's hard to answer. Fictional story based on a public domain course.
