Unless he's using the course to, he's adapting it and turning it into something that's not the public domain version based on the lessons in it. So maybe he's got some extra comments. I think you're right. I think you should answer this question. Well, I think. You're on fiction, right? Okay, so you're exactly right. So you should answer the question because you clearly understand it better than I do. I think broadly, there would be some multi-page before the opt-in or a single long page. And it would create enough tension that the person or the people that would be interested in said thing would then opt-in, at which point, I think the RBS could then introduce them to the idea that you've done. And then at the end of the RBS, it could transition into a full, so proper series that unpacks this thing. I suppose you could do it all in a single RBS. I mean, there's no reason why you couldn't. I just, you know, just functionally inside of the email system, I'd probably keep an RBS and a so proper series separately as separate. So then it makes it easier to modify it to change. So the RBS could be changed independently of the so proper series. So I would have them separate, even though the experience that somebody is going through may be consistent, may be the same like one long linear thing. I would keep them separate inside of the ESP just to make it easier for you to edit and change and do what not. And then obviously a hard bridge to the course, yeah. Yeah, I don't really have anything to add to this other than when I call the house of pizza metaphor. So in order to know what the metaphor is, you have to understand the backstory. I grew up in a very small town, population 15, from junior high on population 1500. Somebody opened a pizza place when I was, I believe it was when I was in ninth grade, summer when I was entering my freshman year of high school, that pizza place changed hands at least 10 times over the years before the building eventually burned down. That was the only thing that got people to stop buying the pizza place and trying to make it work. It would not have taken anybody who bought that place more than about 10 minutes with a pencil and a piece of paper, and you don't even need a calculator. But you could figure out in about 10 minutes that the limits of what that business was likely to produce and the demands required to produce that. It would be very easy to see that that business was never going to get somebody to a point where it made sense financially. So I don't know if this business that you're describing will or will not make sense financially, but when you are selling something that people can get for free, you are automatically starting with, you're starting what's just saying with a disadvantage. So it's not a perfect fit to the house of pizza metaphor, but I would encourage you before you put all of the work into the creation required to make this happen, to sell something that somebody can buy, somebody can just go get for free. Maybe consider an alternative where the thing that you sell eventually isn't available for free. Now that doesn't mean you, it could very well be that you just change how you're thinking about it. So you can say, so the fictional story is based on a public domain course, but you mentioned later that you're thinking about selling that course. Don't sell that course. Sell your course inspired by what you've learned in that course or reference it if it has some sort of like, I mean, I remember years ago, someone did a really good course on Napoleon Hill's book, Thinking Grow Rich, and it was very well done. That book is in the public domain as well, but you didn't need the book because the point of the course was that the book has so much depth and there's so much nuance. And this is part of the mythos of Thinking Grow Rich is that you're going to have an aha moment at some time and you have to have that aha moment to really get it. And this course was like how to maximize your understanding of the book so that you could have that experience. So the book just became a vehicle. And the vehicle was the thing, that's what drove the energy, but anybody could get that book in the public domain very easily. So it was never about the value that was being offered was never about the book. The value proposition was that this book is this treasure, but if you just read the book, you're not going to get the real value from it. And the course is the way to extract all the value out of it. So I would suggest to you so that you don't paint yourself into a corner when inevitably someone's either going to explicitly say to you or implicitly make the choice on their own, why would I pay you for something that I can go get for free? The answer is you can't. You can't get what I'm offering for free because I'm not offering the course. I'm offering this thing that extracts the value that you can't get from just going through the course. And here's why I know that. Like it's all in how you subtly place those two things. I would add that one thought to the question. We did it. We did it in two and a half hours too. Wow, what's a record? Guest. Another record. So this is bittersweet, right? This is the last Q and A call for the live workshop. We do next week, we will record workshop content and we will record a review. So please submit. If you want something reviewed, please submit it last next week. I think I'll close with two things and hand off to Andre. So the first thing is, I can't say thank you enough.
